title: M5 MacBook Pro vs M4 Pro Refurbished – Best Value MacBook for 2025!
transcript: Welcome back to the channel everyone. If you're thinking about upgrading your MacBook in 2025, today's video might just save you hundreds of dollars and even help you get more power for less money. We're comparing the brand new M5 MacBook Pro with the refurbished M4 Pro MacBook Pro, both straight from Apple. One costs 1599 and the other 1,799. But the performance difference, you'll be surprised. Before we dive in, make sure to hit that like button, subscribe, and turn on notifications so you never miss out on the latest tech breakdowns and smart buying guides. Here's the deal. The M5 MacBook Pro starts at $1,599 for the base model. Meanwhile, a refurbished M4 Pro MacBook Pro from Apple's own certified store is $1,69.99, just $100 more. Now, don't be scared of the word refurbished. Apple's refurbished models come fully tested with a brand new battery, new outer shell, and a full one-year warranty. You can even add Apple Care Plus, just like a brand new machine. So, effectively, you're getting a like new MacBook, only smarter and possibly much faster. Let's get to what really matters, performance. In Geekbench 6, the M5 shows small gains in single core performance, but the M4 Pro crushes it in multi-core tests, 20,200 beunes for 17900. That means faster processing when multitasking, compiling code, or running heavy workloads. The M4 Pro packs 12 CPU cores, eight performance, and four efficiency, while the M5 only has 10. So, more muscle where it counts. In Blender rendering tests, the M4 Pro's 16 core GPU outperforms the M5's 10 core GPU, making it much better for creators who edit videos, render 3D graphics, or use apps like Final Cut Pro and Da Vinci Resolve. Even in Final Cut Pro exports, the M4 Pro finishes a 6-minute 4K project in just 25 seconds, while the M5 takes 44 seconds. That's almost twice as fast. And when it comes to coding, the M4 Pro compiled an Xcode project in 92 seconds compared to the M5's 139 seconds. Developers, that's a clear win. In everyday use, web browsing, light editing, office work, both machines feel equally smooth. The M5 does have one edge, better battery efficiency. Thanks to its increased number of efficiency cores, you'll likely get an extra hour or two of use. But if you're doing creative work, programming, or gaming, that power gap will quickly become noticeable. In Cyberpunk 2077 testing, both hovered around 80fps, showing Apple's making great progress in game optimization, but the M4 Pro still handles sustained loads better, keeping cooler under pressure. The M4 Pro also gives you 24 GI of unified memory compared to just 16 GI on the base M5. You could upgrade the M5 to 24 GB, but Apple charges an extra $200 for that, making it $1,999, which is $100 more than the M4 Pro that's already stronger. While the M5 uses PCIe 5.0 storage for slightly faster read to write speeds, the difference isn't huge in real life. And in terms of Mac OS updates, expect about 6 years of support for the M4 Pro and 7 years for the M5. Basically, both will stay relevant for a long time. So, here's the bottom line. For $100 more, the refurbished M4 Pro MacBook Pro gives you more cores, more RAM, and nearly double the performance in creative and pro tasks. If you care about power, performance, and value, go with the M4 Pro. If you prioritize battery life, and portability, the M5 is a great everyday machine. What would you choose? Drop your thoughts in the comments below. And if you found this comparison helpful, hit that like button, subscribe for more detailed tech insights, and share this video with a friend who's shopping for a new MacBook. Thanks for watching, and as always, stay smart, stay curious, and I'll see you in the next one.

So, he still does everything you need.  But if you have specific applications that require more graphics performance or more processor power, then you will need a different chip.  The Pro chip offers a bit more processing power, but above all, significantly more graphics.  In other words, as soon as you do anything with video editing, or maybe even anything with gaming, then the ProT chip becomes immediately useful. And the Max chip is really only for enthusiasts, because it takes things a step further , much, much more in the graphics area.  And that's why it's actually overthopped for many people out there. You can't possibly do enough video editing to need this much graphics power .  You can't possibly do enough 4K gaming to need that much power.  Actually, the Max chip is really only for pure enthusiasts, so the Pro and the Basic are more relevant for you .  So you're the occasional video editors who might cut something here, cut something there, then maybe edit a photo here, and spend most of your time just on the web.  You might use Google Docs, or perhaps Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, or any of these programs. Then the M5 chip, either when it eventually comes to the MacBook Air (in which case it would also be the M4), or in the Pro model, depending on your requirements and budget, is absolutely the right chip for you.  But as soon as you realize, ah, I like to do 4K video editing, I'm editing a lot in parallel, I might do some programming in the future, then maybe invest a few more euros and get a pro version of a chip.  We'll get to the chip version in a moment .  So, whether you should buy the M5 Pro at some point, or rather an M3 Pro, we'll get to that in a moment .  The Maxchip is really for pure enthusiasts, and people who need the performance usually know they need the Max. If you don't know for sure whether you need the Max , then you don't need it. Okay, so now that we've gone over what differentiates the Pro from the Max from the regular one, let's briefly discuss which chip you should buy in that version.  So, the M3, or maybe the M4 Pro, or maybe the M5?  Which one do you think is the best?  And that's where you can really save money, because a new chip comes out every year, but the benchmark we're operating on is extremely high.  Even the M1, which is now 5 years old, still delivers really good performance today, and especially the M1 Pro or M1 Max, still delivers as much performance as some Windows computers don't.  In other words, you're at a high level either way.  Whether you choose an M3 or an M5, you'll probably hardly notice the difference, because if you need more, you'd rather go for a Pro version.  And personally, I have an M4 Pro MacBook Pro, which I recorded and unboxed here on my channel as a debut video a year ago, and I will never switch.  I 'll wait quite a while, probably until the M7 Pro comes out, before I replace this MacBook, simply because the performance is already so high .  In other words, you don't need to be afraid at all of maybe choosing an older model.  The M3 chips in particular were built very efficiently, while the M4 chips focused a bit more on power.  With the M5, it seems to be somewhere in the sweet spot.  However, this does not mean that the M4 chip has poor battery life, because basically all M chips are super energy efficient based on the architecture they are built on .  You can think of Mtips compared to Windows chips a bit like a diesel car versus an electric car. Both can ultimately achieve very, very high performance and very high speeds, but the electric car is simply much more efficient, quieter, and has lower fuel consumption, and that's a perfect fit, because it's the same with the mchips and the diesel engine, which can never deliver what the electric car delivers because they are simply built differently physically .  And that's exactly the case with processors as well; unfortunately, all Windows computers will never be able to compete with the energy efficiency of an Mchip.   So now you have to decide: do you buy the electric car with 300 hp M3 or the electric car with 500 hp M5?   They are all super fast, they all have more power than you need in everyday life, but maybe you'd like to have 500 hp.  I hope this K analogy hasn't confused you all .  I hope it has become a little clearer now that we are operating at a super high level of performance here, one way or another .  And whether you choose the M5 or the M3, you probably won't notice the difference in everyday life .  So, if you're currently looking, take a look around. Take a look at what cool offers might be available here and there.  Maybe there 's something there that's a really good deal.  Something around €1200 where you can get an M3 Pro or something similar. These are excellent deals. The MacBook Pros in this price range simply offer an incredible amount of extras.  So, without the chip.  The device is the same as it was 3 years ago, but it 's already a really, really good device.  Completely independent of the processor.  The MacBook Pro has a superb display, a great feel, great battery performance, and also good cooling that isn't too loud .  In other words, you can't really go wrong with the MacBook Pro.  You just have to decide: are you okay with buying an older model sometimes, or do you want to keep your device for the next 5 years?  Even then, the M3 would probably still do everything.  But of course I can understand if you say you prefer to buy the newest model.   So let 's get to my three recommendations for you.  For the student, the casual user who does things here and there, but is often on the go and writes term papers, the MacBook Air with an M4 chip is just right .  You're looking at roughly €1000.  This is still expensive for students, of course, but it's a good investment.  The device holds its value very well and you can work with it effectively .  For the power-to-price ratio, if you need a little more performance , I would recommend either the M3 Pro, the M4 Pro, or, if it comes out at some point, the M5 Pro, depending on how comfortable you are with buying an older model .  The Pro chips strike a really nice balance between high performance, energy efficiency, and enough headroom if you ever want to try out some things.  And the price is really quite reasonable.  You'll pay roughly a few hundred euros more in price compared to the standard model, but it's generally really worth it. I have a little difficulty recommending the M5 Standard MacBook Pro to many people.  It's generally a good laptop, of course, but you can get a MacBook Air with an M4 processor for a few hundred less. In other words, if you are a standard user, meaning you want a standard chip, you could theoretically also buy the M4 MacBook Air .  Of course, you won't get the MacBook Pro case with the display, but it's still a good device.  But if you simply need a bit more power and want the MacBook Pro feel, then you can buy a MacBook M3 Pro for the same price, or probably an M4 Pro for the same price as well. I would always prefer the Pro version of an older generation to the M5 version without Pro.  And the only exception is actually everyone who works on artificial intelligence.  You might benefit from the M5 neural chips; the others have them too, but they are becoming increasingly important and are now coming into play more and more .  Therefore, the M5 tends to benefit more from this than the other models.  I hope this video has n't confused you even more, but has clarified a bit which chip in which MacBook best suits you. If you don't want to miss any more videos like this , please subscribe, I would really appreciate it, and also leave a like if you generally liked the video .  Feel free to write in the comments which MacBook you want to buy.  Do you perhaps already have one and say, "I wo n't upgrade for the next few years," like me, for example? Or do you say, " Ah, I'm currently looking for one?"  If you have any questions that remain unanswered after this video, then leave them in the comments.  I actually answer most of the questions in the comments.  So, if you have any further questions that weren't addressed in the video , I'll gladly answer them in the comments.  Give me a little time if I don't reply immediately.  Otherwise, see you in the next video.  Thank you so much for watching and see you next week.

title: Apple M6 – ¿El ÚLTIMO Apple Silicon de Historia?
transcript: In 2020, Apple surprised the world with the M1, its first processor based on ARM architecture.  Until that time, Macs relied on Intel processors, but the switch to their own solution was revolutionary.  The new chip was a system-on-a-chip, a complete system that integrated CPU, GPU, Neural Engine, multimedia controllers, and memory into a single package.  This unprecedented integration in desktop computers eliminated bottlenecks by allowing all blocks to use the same unified memory, and being manufactured with a modern, low-power lithography, it offered three times more performance and 50% more battery life than Intel-based Macs. Furthermore, the MacBook Air with M1 operated without a fan, which is why it was silent and MacOS was perfectly adapted to the new hardware.  Since then, Apple has continued to update its range with new generations M2, M3, M4 that improve efficiency and performance, but no longer replicate the initial revolution. Elementos maintained the eight- core design and employed TSMC's N5P process, which is 5 nanm.  The gain was around 10% against M1.  The M3, launched in 2023, moved to the number of 3 nanometers with 25 billion transistors.  But again the leap was moderate, more frequency and a GPU with dynamic cache, although without the expected impact. These evolutions demonstrate that after a big leap like the M1, improvements become more gradual.  But the story changed with the M4, a chip that Apple first introduced in the iPad Pro in spring 2024 and then in the MacBook Pro and Air last fall.  Here there was significant progress.  The M4 increased the CPU cores to 10, with four performance cores and six efficiency cores.   It added ray tracing hardware and increased memory bandwidth to 120 GB per second compared to the M3's 100 GB .  Thanks to that superior bandwidth, the improved multimedia engine, MacBook Pros with M4 handle heavy workloads better, and the unified memory base starts at 16 GB.  That's double the M3 models that started from 8 GB.  Apple also moved from the N3B process to N3E.  It is a more efficient and cheaper one that reduces layers and improves efficiency, resulting in much higher performance per core.  In Geekbench, the M4 reached about 3,750 points on a single thread, almost double that of the M1 and 25% more than the M3.  In terms of multitasking, the improvement exceeds 60%. Users who had kept their machines running on the M1 finally found a reason to upgrade.  The new MacBook Pro with M4 Max or M4 Pro offered configurations of up to 128GB of memory and 40-core GPUs geared towards professional 8K and 3D video. However, devices with M4 Pro or M4 Max are very expensive and their design is evolutionary.  Many opted for a MacBook i with M4, which, with passive cooling, performs excellently for everyday tasks.  In October 2025, the M5 recently arrived and marked the beginning of a new phase.  This chip uses an N3P node, as it is the third generation of 3 nanm and maintains a configuration of 10 CPU cores and 10 GPU cores, but adds a 16-core neural engine with AI accelerators in each graphics core.  According to Apple, the M5's GPU is up to 1.6 times faster than the M4's, and the CP offers 20% more multi-stealth performance. Unified memory bandwidth now exceeds 150 GB per second, almost 30% more than in the M4, allowing recording with local models and complex 3D scenes without bottlenecks. In addition, laptops with M5 maintain around 24 hours of battery life thanks to their energy efficiency.  Well, Apple's message is clear.  The fifth generation of Apple Silicon not only increases power, but is designed for the AI ​​era, hence the multiplier effect on the neural engine and the significant increase in memory bandwidth.  While Apple was shutting down the development of the M5, the focus quickly shifted to the M6.  Well, it's the first Apple chip that will use TSMC's 2 nanm process.  This technological leap will allow for a higher transistor density, better energy efficiency, and significantly superior performance.  The key change will be the new WMCM packaging. This translates as an offer level multiip module that will replace the previous system used since M1.  Thanks to this new system, the CPU, GPU, memory, and Neural Engine will be more integrated, increasing internal bandwidth and reducing latency.  In practice, the M6 ​​will offer better Wio performance and much faster communication between its components.  The GPU is expected to achieve up to 40% more power than the M5, and the Neural Engine is expected to double its cores, from 16 to 32, clearly focusing on I and machine learning tasks.  Apple has also reserved a large portion of TSMC's initial capacity to manufacture at least four 2-nano chips in 2026, including the A20 and the M6 ​​series. So the M6 ​​will not only be an evolution of the M5, but a new structural generation of Apple Silicon focused on deep, local integration.  And from the M6 ​​onwards, the question arises about the future of Apple Silicon as miniaturization approaches physical limits.  The semiconductor industry recognizes that at 2 nanometers, Mora's law begins to lose its force.  Each node reduction offers less profit than the previous one, and design and manufacturing costs skyrocket.  Apple has already delayed the adoption of 2 nanm to the M6 to better capitalize on the 3 nanm process, and all indications are that the M7 and M8 will continue to exploit the same lithography with innovations in 3D packaging. Technologies such as system-on-a-chip and WMCM itself allow chips to be stacked vertically, joined by silicon interposers, and the distances between computing and memory units to be reduced, improving efficiency without the need for smaller transistors.   In addition, there are modular chiplets that allow for the separate manufacturing of CPU, GPU, or AI blocks and their combination according to the product range, and high- bandwidth memories that could be integrated into future on-chip systems to accelerate AI workloads.  Another line of evolution will be specialization, because instead of increasing the CPU frequency, Apple could develop specific inference units for its dedicated computer vision controller services or engines for future augmented reality devices .  In fact, rumors about the M6 ​​suggest separating the CPU and GPU in the Pro and Max models, which will allow each block to be sized separately to optimize power and consumption.  Finally, Apple will continue to leverage its advantage of controlling hardware and software by designing its own operating system and applications, allowing it to better extract every architectural improvement and ensure that a change in the GPU or the neural engine has an immediate impact on Final Cut Pro, Logic, or future AI services.  So when we reach the physical limit of chip miniaturization, probably around 1 nanometer, it will no longer be possible to continue increasing power by only reducing the size of the transistors.  But that won't mark the end of innovation, but rather the beginning of a new stage of innovation, okay? The future of Apple Silicon and the entire processor industry will move towards greater intelligence in design rather than smaller chips. We will see the adoption of heterogeneous architectures and 3D chiplets, where several stacked modes, such as CPU, GPU and memory, will work as a single system with almost instantaneous internal communication. Dedicated accelerators will also be available for specific tasks such as AI, rendering, and video compression, increasing efficiency without the need for higher frequency or heat.  Apple will also promote total integration between devices where the chips of Mac, iPhone, and Vision Pro work together as a distributed supercomputer within the ecosystem.  And further on, new materials and paradigms will emerge, from graphene to neuromorphic chips, quantum chips that will completely change the way we think about computing.  I also have videos about all those technologies; they'll be here on the channel.  I hope you enjoyed this little investigation, and I'll see you in the next one.

title: iPad Pro M5 : ce Détail qui Change TOUT !
transcript: Apple has just released its brand new iPad Pro M5 and I've been able to get my hands on it for a few days now .  And let me make it clear right away, this video is not at all a sponsorship or a loan.  I bought this iPad myself and in this video, we're going to see what's really new on this iPad Pro M5 and especially see if it's worth upgrading to this new generation. Before we begin, please remember to like, comment and subscribe to the channel if you haven't already.  That helps me a lot.  Okay, let's go. Showtime. Last year, Apple had already blown us away with the iPad Pro M4.  It really has insane power, an ultra-thin design and frankly, I loved the 11 and 13 inch models that I tested.  And they were already so good that when I saw the M5 arrive, I wondered what they could really improve on that?  So this year, there is no visual revolution but a real internal revolution.  The heart of the change is of course the new M5 chip manufactured using a 3 nanom process.  It is ultra- efficient and, above all, energy-saving. We now have a 10-core CPU, 4 performance cores and 6 efficiency cores, and an even more powerful GPU with advanced AI capabilities.  And what does that look like in numbers ?  3D Pro rendering with retracing is up to 6.7 times faster than the M1 chip.  Video transcoding is 6 times faster and finally video upscaling by RIA is 3.7 times faster.  In short, it's a rocket.  So yes, Apple compares it to the M1 chip, but that makes sense, nobody changes their iPad every year.  But if you now work in photography, video, sound, AI, then this new generation can really save you time. But what about AI in all of this? Apple has gone all out with its M5 chip on artificial intelligence.  But let's be honest, today Apple's artificial intelligence, Apple Intelligence, is still a bit shaky.  Personally, I find that Chat GPT or Gemini are much more efficient on a daily basis. Also, tell me in the comments if you use Apple Intelligence and if so, what do you do with it?  Honestly, I'm very curious to hear your feedback.  What about RAM and storage on this iPad?  We now go up to 12 GB of RAM on the 256 and 512 models and 16 GB for the 1 T and 2 Tra versions. Storage is also twice as fast in both reading and writing. As a result, if you transfer large files, whether video, photo, etc., between your external hard drives and your iPad, it's super fast.  The iPad Pro M5 features the new M1 network chip compatible with WiFi 7 and Bluetooth 6. Apple therefore promises better stability, especially for Airdrop or connection sharing.  And on the mobile network side, we have the new C1X modem which is up to 50% faster and more economical.  Small but welcome additions.  Another very cool new feature is that the iPad Pro can now drive an external display up to 120Hz with adaptive sync.  That's really great for scrolling, for gaming. Everything really becomes more fluid and responsive.  And it's really cool that they added it.  Another piece of good news: fast charging is coming.  Finally, Apple announces 50% in 30 minutes for the 11-inch model and 35 minutes for the 13-inch model.  However, to take advantage of it, you will obviously need compatible cables and chargers .  In terms of design, there's no real revolution this year. We still have that square, understated and elegant design.  Both models therefore retain the 11 and 13 inch format, a recycled aluminum chassis and the same dimensions and weight.  The screen is still as magnificent as ever. Both models have an Ultra Retina XDR OLED display which is composed of two high-LED panels in tandem.  The result is extreme contrasts, crazy brightness and amazing colors.  The screen also supports a refresh rate of 10 to 120 Hz. It also features Hole Tone, which remains perfectly readable in direct sunlight.  And if you go even further, the 1 Terra and 2 Terra models can have nano-textured glass to reduce reflections.  And here, the result is a little less vibrant but it's super pleasant for working outdoors.  As for accessories, there are no new additions this year.  We're keeping the excellent Magic Keyboard Apple Pencil Pro, accessories that clearly transform the iPad experience into a mini MacBook.  I tested them last year during my review of the iPad Pro M4.  These are really great products.  You can go there with your eyes closed.  Now, as for the camera and audio, well, there's not really any change.  We still have a 12-megapixel 1.8 rear sensor with a 12-megapixel front camera.  Now available in landscape mode, compatible with Center Stage and Face ID.  Too bad, I would have liked to have the latest features that we have on the iPhone 17 Pro, but unfortunately it won't be for this version .  For video, you can still film in 4K at 60 frames per second with the Pro and an audio system with four speakers plus four studio microphones.  In short, all this to say that nothing is changing.  And what about the price? This year, we have a very pleasant surprise as prices are falling slightly.  These iPads are available in silver and space black starting from €1119 for the 11-inch and €1469 for the 13-inch model.  So this year, I took advantage of it, I decided to change the format and change the colors. So, I got the 11-inch silver model, which I think is really nice.  So, should you buy the iPad Pro M5?  If you already have an M3 or M4, honestly, keep them.  But if you 're still on an M1 or M2 chip and you're working on heavy tasks, for example creation, editing, AI, then yes, the M5 can really make a difference.  It is even thinner, lighter, more powerful with a truly sumptuous screen and top-notch connectivity.  In short, it's a productivity monster, perhaps even too powerful for most of us.  This video is already finished. I hope you liked it.  If so , feel free to like, comment and subscribe to the channel if you haven't already.  With that, we'll see you very, very soon for new videos. Alright, bye!

via a wireless USB dongle Plugged directly into the M9 monitor And that’s how I set up my MacBook at home Clean, minimal, and enough for all my daily work needs Now let’s talk about the MacBook interface Before that, there’s a small adjustment I need to make This is the MacBook interface when I’m connected to an external monitor It looks a bit wider So to give you a better idea of what it normally looks like on the 14-inch MacBook screen I’ll switch to larger text This way, the folder icons, toolbar, and app interface sizes Will look closer to the actual experience when using a MacBook The first highlight when it comes to the interface Is the new operating system – macOS 26 Tahoe With the transparent Liquid Glass design Similar to iOS or iPadOS You can clearly see this style in the menu bar Control Center, and app icons Besides that, I also noticed some small design changes For example, in Finder Now the window corners are more rounded And some app icons have been redesigned Like the trash bin icon here And you can also change folder colors With just a few simple steps in the settings Go to Appearance, then select Folder Color For example, I chose a dark gray color to match my desktop wallpaper This wallpaper I downloaded from a website called Unsplash A platform with tons of beautiful, free, high-quality wallpapers You can go there to find the ones you like for your MacBook You can also customize icon and widget styles For example, switch to transparent mode I have that mode enabled on my iPhone Air But for my MacBook, I prefer to keep the default look Another change is the app launch interface Previously, it used Launchpad and opened full screen Now when you click on apps It appears as a small window in the middle of the screen Which looks cleaner and more elegant And one thing I find really cool Is that when you press Escape The apps window shrinks into Spotlight This is also one of the biggest upgrades in macOS 26 Because Spotlight is now optimized Making your searches faster and smarter Not just for apps or folders But also for commands And clipboard history Help me save what I copied earlier The Control Center in macOS 26 has also been redesigned With a transparent, rounded look similar to iPhone and iPad In addition to the default options I’ve added a few custom buttons Like tablet mode Auto-hide dock Screen recording And background sounds Here I set the default to rain sounds Sometimes when I want to focus on studying or working without music I just turn on natural background sounds right here And if you want to change the default sound Go to Accessibility Find Audio and select your preferred background sound I also added a shortcut button to enable Screen Saver Here I use an app called Fliqlo Which turns your screen into a clock when the Screen Saver is on I’ll include the download link in the description Speaking of the menu bar, especially in the top right corner There are a few new icons when upgrading to macOS 26 For example, Apple Intelligence Now I can chat directly with Siri through this icon Or simply by pressing the Command key twice Some icons like Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and Battery remain the same As for sound settings I noticed a clear change in the volume display Previously, when adjusting the volume It appeared in the middle of the screen Now it shows up in the top-right corner instead Looking much cleaner and more refined And here I have a few third-party apps I’d like to share First is the popular GoTiengViet app I think any Vietnamese Mac user already knows about this one Next is Scroll Reverser An app that reverses scrolling direction And lets you set separate scrolling behavior for mouse and trackpad I’m used to using a mouse Therefore, you would typically scroll the roller in the direction shown on the screen That means when you scroll down, the page will also slide down Tuy nhiên thì trên MacBook But on the MacBook trackpad, it’s the opposite When you swipe up, the page scrolls down So this app helps me separate the two Keeping natural scrolling on the trackpad And reverse scrolling on the mouse Just like I’m used to Next is MonitorControl Lite Which allows me to adjust my external monitor’s brightness When using only the MacBook I can change brightness easily with F1 and F2 But when connected to an external monitor Like this Smart Monitor M9 You have to use the monitor’s remote to adjust brightness Which is quite inconvenient So thanks to this app I can just drag the slider left or right Just like when adjusting volume And the external monitor brightness changes immediately I highly recommend this app For anyone who regularly uses an external monitor like I do On my main screen, I have a few widgets Mostly Apple’s default ones Like Calendar, Weather The Journal app And Reminders Speaking of Journal This is a built-in journaling app added in macOS 26 Perfect for those who like journaling Or recording daily thoughts like me In the app, I divide my journal into different lists For example, Morning Pages, Evening Journal Or small notes around specific topics Along with a streak-tracking widget Journal also gives you daily prompt questions To inspire your writing The best part is that it’s completely free And available by default after updating to macOS 26 You should definitely give it a try As for the other two widgets These are synced from my iPhone The one with daily quotes is the TIDE app An app for sleep, focus, and meditation With lots of natural sounds and relaxing music It’s the app I use most often every day From doing morning meditation Setting a 20-minute nap timer Playing ambient sounds while studying or working To tracking my sleep at night On my MacBook, I use this widget As a small daily reminder So every time I open my laptop, I see a new inspiring quote And the last widget with four small circles Is a Habit Tracker app There are many similar apps on the App Store But I chose this one because it’s simple Easy to use, and very affordable I even bought the lifetime premium version Here I track four daily habits Including meditation or yoga Writing my journal, reading, and expense tracking Once I finish a habit, I just tap it and it fills in Meaning that habit’s done for the day Basically, there are some iPhone apps That you can bring their widgets onto your MacBook screen Speaking of that, I just remembered a really interesting and convenient detail That I discovered when upgrading to macOS 26 on both MacBook and iPhone Now, when I start a timer using the Forest app on my iPhone Air The countdown automatically appears in the MacBook’s menu bar It’s just a small detail But I personally find it super useful And about the folders on the desktop I divide them into three main ones Personal, Work, and Media Whenever there are temporary files or new downloads I move them into the corresponding folder So everything stays neat and easy to find Instead of cluttering the desktop Now, let’s take a look at the apps I have in the MacBook Dock. Most of them are apps I use every day, including both third-party apps and some default ones like Finder, Settings, Apps, or Photos. I’ll start with Apple’s Reminders app. This is a to-do list app that I’ve been using for over a year now. The reason I decided to stop using third-party apps and switch back to Reminders is because of its convenience in syncing between Apple devices. And especially, I can track my task lists directly on Apple Calendar. Here, I divide my Reminders lists into four main categories: Personal, Work, Study, and Finance. And since upgrading to macOS 26, the Reminders app now has a very useful new feature — it automatically categorizes tasks into different groups, and even suggests names for those groups. Usually, I’ll write my to-do list at the start of the day or the night before on my MacBook, because typing is faster and more convenient, then I’ll use my iPhone to review and make small edits if needed. For short work notes or bits of information I want to save and quickly search later, I use Apple Notes. For example, product links I use, or playlists I plan to use in my vlogs. This is Notion — an all-in-one app that helps me manage many aspects of life and work. I started using Notion in 2020, initially just for note-taking and knowledge storage during my master’s studies. Sau đó thì dần mở rộng ra cho nhiều mục đích khác nhau Later on, I expanded it for many other purposes — for example, creating an online library to save the books I’ve read or am currently reading, or managing my personal finances, subscriptions, and even savings and investments. In particular, Notion is also my main tool for managing work and creating YouTube content. As of now, it’s been five years using this workflow in Notion, and I’m still completely satisfied with it. For each video project, I create a dedicated page, name it after the tentative title, and store all the voiceover scripts inside. I use Notion on my MacBook about 85 to 90% of the time — mostly for note-taking and writing content. On iPhone or iPad, I mainly use it for quickly checking content when I’m away from my laptop, or to review the script when filming a vlog. I made a full Notion tutorial series so you can check that out for tips on how to use the app on your computer. Next to Notion is a very familiar icon — ChatGPT. But here’s something interesting: there’s actually no standalone ChatGPT app for MacBook. So where does this ChatGPT come from? The answer is Safari. I open Safari, go to the ChatGPT website, then look up at the Menu Bar, select File → Add to Dock. And just like that, you have a ChatGPT web app that you can open directly from the Dock, instead of opening a browser first. And a small tip — you can do the same thing with any website you use frequently, so you can open it instantly with just one click from the Dock. Speaking of Safari, it’s the default browser on macOS. Even though I think it’s great, it’s not the one I use every day. Google Chrome isn’t either. My main browser is Dia Browser — the icon right next to ChatGPT. This is an AI browser with an integrated chat sidebar on the right. When you click it, a small chat box opens. You can ask anything, just like ChatGPT, but it’s even more convenient because the chat box stays inside your current tab. There are a lot of useful features this chat bar provides. For example, I can ask Dia to write an email reply based on the writing tone I’ve pre-set. On YouTube, Dia can automatically summarize video content, and even break it down by timestamps for easier navigation. On Google Maps, I can open the chat bar and ask things like, “Are there any good coffee shops around this area?” Super convenient, right? And what I love most about Dia Browser is that it can save specific “skills” for different commands. For example, I have a saved skill called “sum” to summarize the current tab’s content. Or recently, while creating my Japan trip itinerary, I used a skill called “jikoku” to summarize train routes when searching on Google Maps. It’s incredibly handy. Overall, this is a very powerful and intelligent browser — perfect for anyone who enjoys automation and productivity. However, Dia’s official website is currently inaccessible in Vietnam, So if you want to download it, you’ll need to use a VPN to change your network region. Speaking of VPNs, I use NordVPN, mainly to switch my connection to Japan. That way, I can access and watch some Japanese TV web programs like Tver, or watch Japanese reality shows on Netflix — since Netflix Vietnam mostly only has movies. And once I’ve switched my network to another country, I can also access Dia Browser’s homepage. I already made a dedicated vlog about this browser, so you can check that video for more details. Mình có Spotify I also have Spotify — a music streaming platform where I can listen to millions of songs, podcasts, and personalized playlists based on my habits and preferences. Recently, my favorite playlists include Demon Slayer soundtracks, Hiroyuki Sawano’s OSTs from Attack on Titan, and when I want to listen to relaxing K-Pop, I usually go for Choi Yu Ree. If I’m in the mood for J-Pop or chill music, I open J-Pop Hits or the Tokyo Joshi playlist. Next are my three Adobe apps for photo and video editing — Premiere Pro, Photoshop, and Lightroom. Adobe Premiere Pro is my main and almost the only software I use to edit all my YouTube vlogs and vertical videos like Instagram Reels. If you ask why I don’t use Final Cut Pro — it’s because I used Windows for many years and got used to working with Premiere Pro long ago. So when switching to Mac, I kept the same workflow for consistency. Besides that, I also installed CapCut on my computer, but I use it mainly for one purpose — its automatic subtitle generator. Basically, it converts speech in videos into text and generates synced subtitles automatically. Premiere Pro also has this feature, but it doesn’t support Vietnamese yet, so I use CapCut for that part instead. For deeper image editing — like compositing, removing backgrounds, or fine adjustments, I use Photoshop. For color correction, I use Lightroom. I’ve saved a few of my favorite color presets, so whenever I edit, I just apply one, and it instantly gives me the look I like. Since we’re talking about content creation tools, let me introduce two other apps I use frequently. The first is Audacity — the app I use to record voiceovers for my vlogs. Just like Premiere Pro, I’ve been using Audacity since my Windows days — probably for almost 15 years. So when I switched to MacBook, I kept using it out of habit, even though Mac has built-in options like QuickTime Player or GarageBand. The other app is Aegisub — a tool for creating and editing subtitles. For YouTube videos with international viewers, I often create subtitles in other languages, and Aegisub is what I use to make them. It exports subtitles in SRT format, which YouTube supports directly when uploading. My basic subtitle workflow goes like this: First, I export the original subtitles from Premiere Pro as an .srt file, then open it with Aegisub. After that, I save the subtitles as plain text, copy all the content to translate it into another language — usually using Google Translate or now ChatGPT — then paste it back into the old .srt file. This keeps all the original timestamps and only changes the language. If you often make multilingual subtitles, Aegisub is definitely worth trying — it’s lightweight, accurate, and allows detailed customization of each subtitle line. For studying and research, I have a few apps on my MacBook. First are the two reading apps: Books and Kindle. Books is Apple’s default reading app. The great thing about it is that I can download eBooks in .epub format, open them directly, and they automatically import into Books and sync across all my Apple devices. Besides Books, I also have Kindle, mainly to find and buy Japanese books through Amazon. Overall, I use both of these reading apps mostly on my iPad rather than on the MacBook. I also have Anki — a famous flashcard app, especially effective for language learning or long-term memorization. The key advantage of Anki is its spaced repetition method, which helps me review cards just before I forget them, so I remember things longer without wasting time. I used Anki a lot when studying for the JLPT Japanese exam, though lately I haven’t used it as much. Then there’s Notability — a note-taking app I use often on my iPad. I downloaded Notability on my MacBook mainly to import PDF files, like textbooks or study materials, and then use them on iPad. Sometimes I also open those documents directly on the MacBook screen, so I can use the iPad purely for note-taking. Finally, let’s talk about the games on my MacBook. I think many of you might be curious about what I play on here. First is Cyberpunk 2077 — a very famous RPG shooter set in a futuristic city where humans can upgrade their bodies with technology and implants. This is the official macOS version released this summer. I must say, the performance on the MacBook Pro M4 Pro chip is amazing — smooth gameplay and stunning graphics. In my opinion, it’s one of the few games well optimized for Mac. If you’re using an M3 or M4 chip, you can absolutely enjoy Cyberpunk 2077 smoothly, both in performance and visuals. Next is Solo Leveling: ARISE — a Gacha RPG game adapted from the well-known webtoon and anime of the same name. The gameplay is super satisfying, with beautiful combat effects. Besides the main character, Sung Jin Woo, I can unlock and play many other characters too. In addition to Story Mode, there are various game modes — like boss battles to collect armor and accessories, time-attack boss challenges, and ranking modes across different servers to compete with other players. In general, the difficulty levels increase gradually, and the rewards revolve around items used for strengthening and leveling up Sung Jin Woo and other characters in the game. I’ve been playing Solo Leveling: ARISE for about a year and a half now. At first, I mainly played it on my iPad, but later also on the MacBook. Technically, this game doesn’t have an official macOS version, so I use a third-party app called PlayCover. This app lets you install and play iOS or iPadOS games directly on Mac. I only use PlayCover for this one game though. Besides that, I also play a few games through Steam. If you’re a gamer, you’re probably already familiar with Steam — the biggest platform for buying and playing games on PC and MacBook. I’ve used Steam since the days I played CS:GO and PUBG, about 7–8 years ago. Now I mostly play relaxing, chill games that also run well on macOS. One of them is InZoi — a life simulation game similar to The Sims 4, but built with Unreal Engine 5, so the graphics are incredibly realistic and detailed. I once let my wife try it, and she spent an entire afternoon just designing one character. The gameplay is quite similar to The Sims 4, but with more features, especially a very polished building and space customization system. Overall, if you enjoy life simulation games — something chill yet creative — InZoi is definitely worth playing. You can also try some other Steam games like Hades, Haste, Neva, or if you’re a cat lover — definitely try Stray. And that’s everything on my MacBook Pro. I hope this sharing helps you discover some new apps and fun games to try on your own MacBook. Right now, Apple has just released the new MacBook lineup with the M5 chip, which offers noticeable performance improvements over the M4 series. However, personally I think the M4 Pro and M4 Max are still excellent choices especially if you edit vlogs, play games, and do heavy tasks like I do. Still, I’m quite curious to see how the M5’s performance compares to the M4. I also uploaded a vlog called “What’s on My iPad” on this YouTube channel. If you’re interested in how I set up my iPad and what apps I use on it, be sure to check it out. As for “What’s on My iPhone Air,” please wait for me a bit longer. Thank you for watching this vlog, and see you in the next video!

title: Apple Watch Ultra 3 Review – I’m NEVER Going Back!
transcript: I never liked the Apple Watch Ultra. It was always too big, too heavy, too ugly. When Apple introduced it in 2022, I was surprised because it didn't look like an Apple product. Mostly for these three reasons. One, its battery life, which was insane for an Apple Watch. I could literally get like 2 days and a half out of it as opposed to one and a half like with my Series 10. And number two, its durability. My Series 10 already had a broken speaker. Sadly, a widespread issue for anyone with a Series 10. >> [bell] [music] [bell] [music and bell] >> If you swim with it, water can get inside of it. And when I get home, I then change it to be a flashlight. something very useful to have for nightly bathroom breaks. All in all, I am super happy with my Ultra 3. Really, my only complaint is that it lags. The control center tends to lag quite often, and I even had issues with the crown not working at all every now and then with both my initial unit and my replacement. This therefore does seem to be a watch OS 26 issue. But having said all of this, I don't regret buying my Apple Watch Ultra 3. Yes, it is a big watch, but I do love having that 3-day battery life or even longer with low power mode. And most importantly, I love having a durable watch that I can rely on no matter the circumstance. As for who should buy this, if you're looking for an Apple Watch with about double the battery life of the regular model and that you also want to use for the next 5 years or so, then definitely go for the Ultra. Also, if you're debating maybe getting the titanium version of the Series 11, I would just go for the Ultra as there's only a $100 to $50 price difference and you get a lot of extra features. So, let me know what do you guys think and stay tuned for my AirPods Pro 3 review coming very soon. I'm Daniel. This is Enough of Tech and I'll see you guys in the next one. Enough of Tech signing out. Cheers.
